project_overview.project_development_objective: SOP I: Introduce sustainable municipal finance and management systems in nine participating PCCs and rehabilitate municipal infrastructure in five participating PCCs. Program Development Objective: Improve the sustainability and livability of nine regionally-balanced Provincial Capital Cities (PCCs).
project_overview.total_financing: US$50 million (IDA Grant: US$25 million; ARTF Grant: US$25 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Residents and businesses of nine participating Provincial Capital Cities (PCCs): Herat, Jalalabad, Kandahar, Khost, Mazar-i-Sharif (Group I - eligible for Component 1 financing), and four additional PCCs to be determined (Group II - eligible for Components 2 and 3 only). Estimated 800,000 urban residents benefiting from project components by project completion.

primary_locations: Four additional PCCs to be determined (North, South, East, West)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: Regional - nine Provincial Capital Cities across four geographic regions of Afghanistan
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - partial. Afghanistan is classified as a fragile and conflict-affected state. The PAD explicitly notes: 'Civilian casualties have reached unprecedented levels in 2017 (10,451 killed or wounded) and 2018 (8,050 in the first 9 months). Some areas of the country remain difficult to access because of insecurity.' The project targets cities where the security situation is less volatile. More than 1.7 million Afghans are internally displaced, and over 2 million have returned since 2015, mostly settling in urban areas.

key_components.description: Financing on a performance basis to support five participating PCCs (Herat, Jalalabad, Kandahar, Khost, Mazar-i-Sharif) in rehabilitation of urgently-needed basic municipal infrastructure. Priority Projects include roads, stormwater drainage, street lighting, public spaces, markets, and bus stops. Financing allocated based on population size and urban built-up area. Performance-based allocation in three cycles: Cycle 1 (5% of allocation upon effectiveness), Cycle 2 (45% upon commitment to prepare Municipal Performance Improvement Plan), Cycle 3 (50% upon MPIP preparation). Women and disadvantaged groups targeted with minimum one gender-focused investment per city.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthen the Municipal Finance Framework
key_components.description: Technical assistance to support GoIRA and nine participating PCCs in building municipal capacity for improved own-source revenue (OSR) collection and design of performance-based fiscal allocation system. Includes: (i) custom-designed OSR Enhancement Strategies and Plans for nine PCCs; (ii) support to IDLG and MOF in designing institutionalized performance-based fiscal allocation; (iii) design and integration of Municipal Finance Database into Urban Management Information System (UMIS).

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Improve the Municipal Management Framework
key_components.description: Support for strengthening municipal planning and management at nine PCCs through technical assistance, training, and equipment/software. Includes: (3.1) Municipal Capacity Building Packages (MCBPs) with Asset Inventory and Management Plans (AIMP), automated AFMIS modules, and budget planning tools for all nine PCCs; (3.2) Strategic Development Frameworks (SDFs) and Capital Investment Plans for four second-generation PCCs; (3.3) Feasibility and design studies for catalytic investment projects; (3.4) Technical assistance for urban policy reforms to MUDH (affordable housing action plan and urban planning framework strengthening).

Support for IDLG in managing CIP implementation. Covers costs of technical consultants for Project Implementation Unit (PIU), office equipment and furnishings, incremental operating costs, project vehicles, and independent verification consultant for monitoring municipal performance improvements. PIU will be stationed in Kabul with support to five PCCs under SOP I, expanding to nine PCCs under SOP II.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Independent Directorate for Local Governance (IDLG), with Deputy Ministry for Municipalities (DMM) as the specific department responsible. IDLG reports directly to the President and has 481 professional and administrative staff at headquarters, 34 Provincial Governorate offices, 376 District Governorate Offices, and 33 Provincial Municipal Offices.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Implementation Unit (PIU) established at IDLG headquarters in Kabul with Project Director, Investment Coordinator, Policy Coordinator, Municipal Capacity Building Coordinator, Procurement Specialists, Financial Management Specialists, Environmental and Social Safeguards Specialists, and Monitoring and Evaluation Officer. City Coordinators with municipal engineering background stationed at each of five PCCs under SOP I. Program Working Group (WG) convened every six months comprising IDLG (Chair), Ministry of Urban Development and Housing (MUDH), Ministry of Finance (MOF), and PCC representatives. Municipal Performance Improvement Plans (MPIPs) signed between IDLG and nine participating PCCs. Coordination with ongoing projects: KMDP (Kabul), UDSP (five PCCs), EZ-Kar (four PCCs), and CCAP (national).
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2017) apply. Standard Procurement Documents used for international competitive procurement. Model Procurement Documents for Afghanistan used for national open competition. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system used for planning and tracking. Two Senior Procurement Specialists and two Procurement Officers recruited under project to support IDLG Procurement Unit. Prior review thresholds apply for high-risk entities. Procurement capacity assessed as 'High Risk' due to limited capacity of IDLG Procurement Unit; mitigation includes dedicated procurement staff and Bank training.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: IDLG responsible for overall M&E. Dedicated Monitoring and Evaluation Specialist (MES) hired to coordinate data collection for Results Framework, monitor performance improvements, and prepare quarterly progress reports. Baseline data collection and monitoring schedule agreed with participating PCCs. Beneficiary surveys conducted at project midterm and completion. Independent third-party monitoring agent contracted by Country Management Unit (CMU) using ARTF funding to conduct field-based verification and monitoring of PCC performance and physical works progress. Quarterly IUFR (Integrated Unaudited Financial Report) submitted to Bank. Annual audited financial statements prepared by Supreme Audit Office in accordance with Cash Basis IPSAS. Gender-disaggregated data collected and monitored. Results Framework includes PDO indicators (beneficiary satisfaction, number of beneficiaries, budgetary improvements, OSR targets) and intermediate results indicators (roads constructed, grievances resolved, design sharing, gender-focused investments, performance-based finance mechanism, asset inventories, SDFs, catalytic investment designs).

identified_risks.risk_description: Risks of political instability, particularly around 2018-2019 elections. Prospects for rapid economic growth and substantial increases in domestic revenue generation are limited. Increasing internal and external pressures could side-track strategic decisions. Weak institutional capacity and limited government ownership of reforms.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: CIP designed flexibly to respond to exigencies. Programmatic approach (Series of Projects) allows for incremental expansion and adjustment. Focus on strengthening municipal capacity and performance-based incentives. Continuous inter-agency consultation and consensus-building. Bank team scaled up in field with dedicated implementation support. Weekly virtual team meetings during preparation phase, continuing during implementation. Real-time monitoring and adaptive management approach.

identified_risks.risk_description: Real GDP growth projected at 2.4% in 2018, moderating from 2.7% in 2017. Growth expected to accelerate to 3.7% by 2021 only if smooth political transition occurs. Population growing at 2.7%, outpacing projected growth. Poverty rate increased from 38% (2011/12) to 55% (2016/17) and expected to remain high. Weak labor demand and security-related constraints on service delivery. Volatile macro-fiscal status.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project aligned with more efficient public expenditure management through technical assistance in public financial management. Performance-based financing framework designed to allocate scarce fiscal resources efficiently and avoid moral hazard. Focus on municipal revenue mobilization and fiscal discipline. Outsourced procurement capacity to support preparation of technically sound civil works packages. Flexibility built into project design to adjust to changing conditions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Institutional arrangements for urban development and municipal management in flux. Recent improvements to legal framework (Urban Development Law 2017, new Municipal Law issued August 2018) but implementation uncertain. Unclear rules governing distribution of central government resources to municipalities. Inadequate infrastructure identified as significant constraint on private investment. Cities underfinanced with little or no regular financial support from central government.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports implementation of new Municipal Law through performance-based municipal finance framework. Technical assistance to IDLG and MOF in designing institutionalized performance-based fiscal allocation. Support for municipal capacity building and systems development. Coordination with USAID on Municipal Development Fund design. Collaboration with UNESCO on cultural heritage management and UNHABITAT on informal settlement upgrading.

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of institutional development challenges. Limited experience with performance-based municipal financing in Afghanistan. Difficulty in identifying and preparing bankable projects in secondary cities. Weak municipal budget execution (only 40% of development budgets executed on average). Variance between planned and actual expenditures.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Programmatic approach (SOP) allows for incremental introduction of reform efforts and establishment of performance standards over time. Building block approach to technical assistance using tried and tested methods within country and other fragile contexts. Positive List of Priority Projects focuses on simplicity and rehabilitation of existing infrastructure to minimize risks. 15% contingency margin in cost estimates for first cycle. Five-year implementation period to account for risks. Outsourced procurement support and design preparation to accelerate procurement cycle. Component 3 provides capacity building support for municipal financial management, budgeting, and asset management.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: IDLG has limited financial management capacity in terms of number and qualification of staff. Weak fixed assets management. Excessive use and delayed liquidation of cash advances. Absence of management reporting system to monitor physical and financial progress. Low budget credibility due to weak planning process. Limited scope and coverage of internal audit with poor quality of audit reports. Non-compliance with external audit recommendations. Procurement capacity assessed as 'High Risk' with 11 newly-hired staff unfamiliar with Bank procedures. Development budget utilization rate less than 50%.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Time-bound mitigation measures agreed with IDLG. Within three months of effectiveness, consulting firm to strengthen financial management capacity including capacity building plan based on assessment. IDLG to adopt and implement Financial Management Manual for IDA and ARTF projects. Chartered accountancy firm engaged under EZ-Kar to conduct semi-annual internal audit of CIP. Two Senior Procurement Specialists and two Procurement Officers recruited under project to support IDLG Procurement Unit and enhance capacity. Bank procurement team to provide training on Bank regulations. Well-equipped PIU with recruitment of key personnel during preparation phase. Outsourcing of sub-project design and tender package preparation. Strong PIU with adequate autonomy and budget to attract qualified consultants. Hands-on training for counterpart implementing agencies. Continuous inter-agency consultation and consensus-building.

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial management risk assessed as 'Substantial' due to weaknesses in IDLG financial management systems. Procurement risk assessed as 'High' due to limited capacity in IDLG Procurement Unit. Weak internal audit and non-compliance with external audit recommendations. Delayed budget approvals (sometimes up to six months into fiscal year). Excessive use of cash advances with delayed liquidation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Financial Management Manual adopted and implemented. Senior Financial Management Specialist and Finance Officer hired under project. Designated Account (DA) established for CIP, denominated in US Dollars, with front-loading based on six-month cash forecast. Quarterly IUFR submitted to Bank. Annual audited financial statements prepared by Supreme Audit Office in accordance with INTOSAI standards and Cash Basis IPSAS. Internal audit by chartered accountancy firm semi-annually. Project Operations Manual (POM) prepared with detailed financial management, procurement, and safeguards procedures. Conditions of disbursement include adoption of POM and signed MOU between IDLG and MOF on Incentive Fund regulatory framework. Prior review thresholds applied for high-risk contracts. Procurement training provided to IDLG staff. STEP system used for procurement planning and tracking.

identified_risks.risk_description: Project classified as Category B for environmental safeguards. Potential for involuntary resettlement impacts under Component 1 (OP 4.12 triggered). Potential environmental impacts from Priority Projects and infrastructure investments. Social impacts from rapid urbanization, displacement, and influx of IDPs and returnees. Climate change vulnerabilities including drought, flooding, and landslides, particularly in informal settlements.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed. Positive List of Priority Projects rules out any involving land acquisition. Detailed screening of sub-projects at pre-selection stage to identify any encumbrances involving involuntary resettlement. Site-specific Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs) and Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) prepared for Priority Projects where relevant. Social screening and scoping report prepared for Priority Projects in five cities. Dedicated Social Safeguards Specialist based at PIU. Community Consultation Manual adapted from KMDP for stakeholder/community consultations. Multi-level Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) established with five hierarchical levels. Beneficiary surveys on draft designs and after completion. Emphasis on vulnerable groups, especially women and marginalized groups. SDFs include dedicated component for environmental impact mitigation and management with climate change considerations. Climate change and natural disaster considerations integrated into capacity building, capital investment planning, and asset management. Estimated 33% of project value provides climate change co-benefits through reduced vulnerability to flooding and landslides.

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited citizen engagement and participation in municipal governance. Weak accountability mechanisms between local government and citizens. Marginalized groups (women, urban poor, IDPs, returnees) have limited voice in development planning. Potential resistance to municipal reforms and performance-based financing.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Citizen engagement (CE) integral to project design. Strategic Development Frameworks (SDFs) highly participatory with multiple stages of consultation with residents, businesses, NGOs, and government agencies. Community consultations on Priority Project identification and design. Women targeted as specific focal sub-group during SDF preparation to understand challenges and identify city-specific solutions. At least one Priority Project investment targeting women in each of five PCCs. Multi-level Grievance Redress Mechanism with community-level, neighborhood-level, district-level, municipality-level, and HQ-level grievance redress committees. Multiple grievance uptake channels (phone, email, complaint forms, suggestion boxes). Grievance logbook at sub-office level and central grievance database for tracking. Community monitoring and beneficiary satisfaction surveys. Gender-disaggregated data collection and monitoring. Specific measures to gather gender-related beneficiaries' data. Minimum 70% of grievances to be satisfactorily resolved within stipulated timeframe. Draft designs for Priority Projects shared with local community/beneficiaries for feedback (100% target).

identified_risks.risk_description: Ongoing conflict and insecurity in Afghanistan. Civilian casualties at unprecedented levels (10,451 in 2017, 8,050 in first 9 months of 2018). Some areas of country remain difficult to access due to insecurity. Mobility constraints for implementation teams. Risk of project disruption due to security incidents.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project targets cities where security situation is less volatile. Innovative implementation support mechanisms including third-party monitoring agent for field-based monitoring to address mobility constraints. Increased Bank team budget for supervision. Expanded field-based team at Country Office with dedicated implementation support staff. City Coordinators and Municipal Engineers at local level. Short and restricted in-country combined missions (at least three per year). Bi-weekly video conferencing with IDLG PIU. Off-shore technical missions and capacity building training. Real-time face-to-face dialogue and monitoring through expanded field-based team. Flexible project design to respond to security exigencies. Realistic assessment of project implementation period accounting for security risks.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Multi-level Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) established with five hierarchical levels: (i) community-level Grievance Redress Committee (GRC); (ii) Gozar/Neighborhood-level GRC; (iii) district-level GRC; (iv) municipality-level GRC; (v) HQ-IDLG-level GRC. GRM unit established with GRM Manager and GRM Analysis Officer. Provincial Authorities nominated dedicated GRM Provincial Focal Points. Multiple grievance uptake channels: phone, email, complaint forms, suggestion boxes at IDLG sub-offices and sub-project offices. Grievance logbook at sub-office level and central grievance database for tracking and review. Grievance management guidance notes developed in accordance with international good practices and national practices (e.g., NSP III). Target: 70% of grievances satisfactorily resolved within stipulated timeframe. Same GRM procedures and unit used for CCAP will be applied for CIP. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) also available for complaints.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender analysis conducted to identify project-relevant gaps between males and females. Specific actions to address gender gaps and improve women's empowerment included in project design. Gender indicators included in results framework with gender-disaggregated data collection and monitoring. Women's mobility and safety in urban public places identified as key gender gap. Women targeted as specific focal sub-group during SDF preparation and consultations to understand challenges and identify city-specific solutions. Minimum one Priority Project investment targeting women required in each of five PCCs. Specific subprojects identified to serve women: rehabilitation and development of public parks exclusively for women, restoration of public markets and women's markets, extension and upgrading of pedestrian walkways, bus stops, and street lighting. At least one gender-focused investment already identified by five PCCs. Gender committees created under municipal framework to increase women's voice in local governance (22 gender committees created to date). Gender-disaggregated beneficiary data collected through surveys. PDO indicators disaggregated by gender: percent of surveyed beneficiaries rating services satisfactory (target 70%), number of urban residents benefiting from project components.
vulnerable_groups: Returnees (Afghan nationals returning from Pakistan and Iran)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity in mind. PAD notes: 'Key lessons from operations in fragile and conflict-affected environments, and specifically drawing from the Bank's two decades of work in urban areas in Afghanistan, which have been integrated into the project's design.' Conflict-induced displacement recognized as major driver of urbanization: 'more than 1.7 million Afghans are internally displaced, and more than 2 million have been returning to Afghanistan – mostly from Pakistan and Iran – since 2015.' Project aims to support host communities in absorbing influx of internally displaced migrants and returnees. Programmatic approach allows for flexibility and adjustment to conflict dynamics. Project targets cities where security situation is less volatile. Community engagement mechanisms (SDFs, consultations, GRM) designed to promote social cohesion and inclusive development. Infrastructure investments aimed at improving livability and economic opportunities to support stability. Performance-based financing framework designed to promote efficient resource allocation and reduce grievances. Gender-focused investments and women's participation in planning designed to promote social inclusion.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan classified as fragile and conflict-affected state. Key fragility factors: (1) Ongoing armed conflict with civilian casualties at unprecedented levels (10,451 killed/wounded in 2017, 8,050 in first 9 months of 2018); (2) Political instability around 2018-2019 elections with declining business confidence and slowing economic activity; (3) Weak institutional capacity and governance, with limited accountability mechanisms; (4) Severe poverty (55% in 2016/17, up from 38% in 2011/12) and weak labor demand; (5) Massive displacement: 1.7 million internally displaced, 2+ million returnees since 2015, mostly settling in urban areas; (6) Weak public financial management and municipal finance systems; (7) Limited central government support to municipalities; (8) Climate vulnerabilities (Afghanistan ranks in top fifth of countries with substantial climate change vulnerabilities); (9) Weak private sector and limited business investment due to inadequate infrastructure; (10) Informal economy and limited tax base. Project designed to address fragility through: institutional strengthening, municipal capacity building, performance-based incentives, infrastructure investment, revenue mobilization, and inclusive governance mechanisms.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security situation volatile with some areas of country difficult to access due to insecurity. Mobility constraints for implementation teams. Project addresses security considerations through: (1) Targeting cities where security situation is less volatile; (2) Engaging third-party monitoring agent for field-based monitoring to overcome mobility constraints; (3) Increased Bank team budget for supervision; (4) Expanded field-based team at Country Office; (5) Bi-weekly video conferencing with IDLG PIU; (6) Short and restricted in-country combined missions (at least three per year); (7) Off-shore technical missions; (8) Realistic assessment of implementation period accounting for security risks; (9) Flexible project design to respond to security exigencies; (10) Contingency margins in cost estimates (15% for first cycle); (11) Outsourced procurement support to accelerate implementation despite security constraints.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - significant displacement and refugee issues. More than 1.7 million Afghans internally displaced due to conflict. More than 2 million Afghan nationals have returned since 2015, mostly from Pakistan and Iran. Majority of returnees (approximately 50%) have settled in urban areas, placing pressure on already rapidly growing cities. Project explicitly designed to support host communities in absorbing influx of internally displaced migrants and returnees. Project aims to improve livability and economic opportunities in nine Provincial Capital Cities to support integration of displaced and returning populations. Infrastructure investments and municipal service improvements expected to benefit IDPs and returnees. Economic opportunities created through construction projects and improved business environment. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared to address any potential involuntary resettlement impacts from project activities. Positive List of Priority Projects rules out any involving land acquisition to minimize resettlement risks. Social Impact Assessment (SIA) conducted to identify key social safeguards concerns. Community consultations designed to ensure responsiveness to needs of vulnerable populations including IDPs and returnees.
